coredata.dc:title: Development of a sensitive phage-mimotope and horseradish peroxidase based electrochemical immunosensor for detection of O,O-dimethyl organophosphorus pesticides
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Ruirui
Zou, Wenting
Zhao, Zhiling
Wang, Guanqun
Guo, Min
Ai, Shiyun
Zhou, Qing
Zhao, Fengchun
Yang, Zhengyou
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a green, harmless and signal-amplified electrochemical immunosensor based on phage-mimotope M31 (C–P-D-G-N-H-V-P-F-C) and horseradish peroxidase (HRP) was constructed for detecting O,O-dimethyl organophosphorus pesticides (OPs). The glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was modified by nitrogen and boron doped carbon quantum dots and graphene oxide (NBCQDs@GO) which can provide sufficient surface area and enhance the conductivity of the electrode. The O,O-dimethyl OPs class specific antibody mAb3C9 was assembled onto the NBCQDs@GO and the phage-mimotope M31 competitively bound to mAb3C9 with OPs. Furthermore, large amounts of anti-M13 mAb-HRP were introduced to the electrode through thousands of binding sites on the capsid of phage. HRP can catalyze 4-chloro-1-naphthol (4-CN) to produce insoluble precipitates (Benzo-4-chlorhexanedione, 4-CD). Hence, the concentration of OPs can be quantified by measuring impedance signal with electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS). Under the optimal detection conditions, the 50% inhibitory concentration (IC50) and limits of detection (LODs) values of 9 O,O-dimethyl OPs were in range of 0.989–4.017 ng/mL and 0.003–0.014 ng/mL, respectively. The recovery rates of spiked OPs in cucumber, cabbage and lettuce were 88.20–112.50% with coefficient of variation from 2.97 to 15.64%. Therefore, the immunosensor showed very good sensitivity and demonstrating potential application for the detection of O,O-dimethyl OPs in food samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phage-mimotope
Horseradish peroxidase
Electrochemical immunosensor
Electrochemical impedance spectrum
Organophosphorus pesticides

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Screening of mAb3C9 phage-mimotopes by phage display
Sensitivity and stability of the immunosensor
Analysis of spiked vegetable samples by the electrochemical immunosensor
Characterizations of NBCQDs@GO composite and GCE/NBCQDs@GO
Optimization of detection conditions for the electrochemical immunosensor
Sensitivity and stability of the electrochemical immunosensor
Analysis of the spiked vegetable samples by the electrochemical immunosensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic of fabrication steps of phage-mimotope and HRP based electrochemical immunosensor.
P-MELISA results of the phage peptide library from each round of panning (A) and 10 phage-mimotopes against 10 ng/mL parathion-methyl (B), and the Amax/IC50 values of the 10 phage-mimotopes (C).

CVs of GCE/NBCQDs@GO (A) and the plot of anodic and cathodic peak currents versus the square root of scan rate (B). The CVs tested was carried out in the solution containing [Fe (CN)6 3−] (5 mM) and KCl (0.1 M) from - 0.2 to + 0.6 V.

Characterization of the fabrication process of phage-mimotope M31 and HRP based electrochemical immunosensor by CV (A) and EIS (B). The CVs tested was carried out in the solution containing [Fe (CN)6 3−] (5 mM) and KCl (0.1 M) from - 0.2 to + 0.6 V with a scan rate of 100 mV/s. The EIS was tested in 10 mM PBS containing [Fe (CN)6 3−/4-] (5 mM), and a potential of 0.2 V and amplitude of 0.005 V were applied. The CV and EIS curves of GCE (a), GCE/NBCQDs@GO (b), GCE/NBCQDs@GO/mAb3C9 (c), GCE/NBCQDs@GO/mAb3C9/BSA (d), GCE/NBCQDs@GO/mAb3C9/BSA/phage-mimotope (e) and GCE/NBCQDs@GO/mAb3C9/BSA/phage-mimotope/anti M13-HRP (f), respectively.
Stability verification of phage-mimotope M31 and HRP based electrochemical immunosensor (n = 3).
The amino acid sequences of 10 phage-mimotopes.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: “M" is short for “mimotope”, and the Arabic numeral stands for the phage clone containing the corresponding “mimotope".

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The sensitivity and cross-reactivity of phage-mimotope M31 and HRP based electrochemical immunosensor and phage-mimotope M31 based P-MELISA (n = 3).
Recoveries of three O,O-dimethyl OPs in vegetable samples by phage-mimotope M31 and HRP based electrochemical immunosensor (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Development of a sensitive phage-mimotope and horseradish peroxidase based electrochemical immunosensor for detection of O,O-dimethyl organophosphorus pesticides

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microbiology, College of Life Science, Key Laboratory for Agriculture Microbiology, Shandong Agricultural University, Taian, 271018, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Microbiology, College of Life Science, Key Laboratory for Agriculture Microbiology, Shandong Agricultural University, Taian 271018, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University, Taian, 271018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University, Taian 271018, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a green, harmless and signal-amplified electrochemical immunosensor based on phage-mimotope M31 (C–P-D-G-N-H-V-P-F-C) and horseradish peroxidase (HRP) was constructed for detecting O,O-dimethyl organophosphorus pesticides (OPs). The glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was modified by nitrogen and boron doped carbon quantum dots and graphene oxide (NBCQDs@GO) which can provide sufficient surface area and enhance the conductivity of the electrode. The O,O-dimethyl OPs class specific antibody mAb3C9 was assembled onto the NBCQDs@GO and the phage-mimotope M31 competitively bound to mAb3C9 with OPs. Furthermore, large amounts of anti-M13 mAb-HRP were introduced to the electrode through thousands of binding sites on the capsid of phage. HRP can catalyze 4-chloro-1-naphthol (4-CN) to produce insoluble precipitates (Benzo-4-chlorhexanedione, 4-CD). Hence, the concentration of OPs can be quantified by measuring impedance signal with electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS). Under the optimal detection conditions, the 50% inhibitory concentration (IC50) and limits of detection (LODs) values of 9 O,O-dimethyl OPs were in range of 0.989–4.017 ng/mL and 0.003–0.014 ng/mL, respectively. The recovery rates of spiked OPs in cucumber, cabbage and lettuce were 88.20–112.50% with coefficient of variation from 2.97 to 15.64%. Therefore, the immunosensor showed very good sensitivity and demonstrating potential application for the detection of O,O-dimethyl OPs in food samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pesticides are used to improve the quality and yield of crops in agriculture, and among them, organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) are widely applied due to their high efficiency, broad spectrum, diversity, and low cost (Ghorbani et al., 2022; J. Wang et al., 2020). However, as an inhibitor of acetylcholinesterase, the extensive or inappropriate use of OPs may bring risk to human health and the environment (Kumar et al., 2020; Milinčić et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2018; Zou et al., 2019). European Union ((EC)396/2005) stipulated that the maximum residue limits (MRLs) of OPs in vegetables were 0.01 ppm, while Chinese National Standard (GB18406-1-2001) stipulated that parathion, parathion-methyl, malathion and phorate shouldn't be detected in vegetables. Therefore, it is the top priority to develop a highly sensitive and selective detection technology of OPs for reducing their harm to human bodies. Up to now, several qualitative and quantitative strategies for OPs have been reported, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (Qian et al., 2020), gas chromatography (Kaur et al., 2019), liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (Yu et al., 2020) and gas chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (Fernández-Cruz et al., 2020). However, these methods usually need relatively complicate procedures or pretreatment prior to analysis, and demand severely in the way of equipment and professional operating technology. As an alternative, immunoassays have the advantages of rapidness, sensitivity and effectiveness, therefore numerous immunoassays have developed for OPs analysis (G. Chen et al., 2021; Hongsibsong et al., 2020; L. Li et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2008; Zhao et al., 2021). The typical OPs detection methods of immunoassay including enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), immunochromatographic assay (ICA) and fluorescence immunoassay (FLIA). Despite many advances have been achieved, there is still exploration for new schemes and strategies to provide higher sensitivity biosensor to evaluate the residual OPs (Rostiyanti and Mubarok, 2021; Tang et al., 2012; Viveros et al., 2006; Wang et al., 2013; Xu et al., 2019).

Electrochemical immunosensor, based on specificity of immune reaction and sensitivity of electrochemical signal transduction, has received intense attention in the field of food safety (Peteu et al., 1996; Piro et al., 2016). Chemosynthetic antigens (CAgs) are vital elements in immunoassay of small molecules. However, during the process of the CAgs synthesis, some highly toxic haptens have been produced, which will seriously endanger the health of experimental personnel. In order to develop an immunoassay, which is environmentally friendly, safe, and convenient to popularize, phage display technology has been widely used to prepare substitutes for CAgs, such as phage-mimotopes (Chen et al., 2019; He et al., 2013; Huang et al., 2021), anti-immune complex peptides (González-Techera et al., 2007; Kim et al., 2009) and anti-idiotypic antibodies (Schulz et al., 2019). At present, phage-mimotope based electrochemical immunosensor has been successfully used to detect ochratoxin (Hou et al., 2018; Lv and Wang, 2020), deoxynivalenol (Yan et al., 2019) and zearalenone (Fang et al., 2019), it may provide a novel concept for OPs detection.

Integrating diverse strategies to effectively improve electrochemical signal has been a research hot topic (Xiong et al., 2020). Nanomaterials (NMs) such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphenes (GNs), metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and nanopolymers (NPs) are widely used due to their large specific surface area, rich active sites, various functions, multiple synthesis methods, and unique optical and electrical properties (Y. Li et al., 2015; Ronkainen and Okon, 2014; Timur and Anik, 2016; W. Wang et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2021). Among them, graphene oxide (GO), not only shows good electrical conductivity, strong hydrophilicity, large specific surface area, but also can ensure covalent linkage with biomolecule such as antibodies, has performed much potential in the field of electrochemical detection of small molecular contaminants (Cao et al., 2017; G. Chen et al., 2021; Sha et al., 2016). Additionally, CQDs have attracted extensive attention of researchers because of their significant advantages, including high fluorescence stability, strong conductivity, easy preparation and so on (Liu et al., 2021).

The aim of this study is to develop a high-sensitive electrochemical immunosensor for the detection of O,O-dimethyl OPs. The glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was modified by CQDs and GO to provide sufficient surface area and enhance the conductivity. The phage-mimotope was used to instead of CAgs and competitively bound to the OPs antibody on the electrode with OPs. Further, large amounts of horseradish peroxidase labelled anti-M13 mAbs (anti M13-HRP) were introduced to the phage, and HRP catalyzed 4-chloro-1-naphthol (4-CN) to produce insoluble precipitate which could greatly amplify the impedance signal (S. Chen et al., 2021). The electrochemical immunosensor may provide a new way for highly sensitive and green detection of small molecular contaminants in food samples.

Nine kinds of O,O-dimethyl OP standards were purchased from Zhongkezhijian Biotechnology Co. Ltd (Beijing, China). Carboxyl functionalized polystyrene magnetic beads (5 mg/mL, diameter of 500–1000 nm) were purchased from Baseline Chromtech Research Centre (Tianjin, China). 1-Ethyl-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbondiimide hydrochloride (EDC·HCl) and N-Hydroxysulfosuccinimide sodium salt (sulfo-NHS) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry (Shanghai, China). Anti M13-HRP were purchased from GE Healthcare. GO was purchased from Nanjing Jicang Nanotechnology Co., Ltd (Jiangsu, China). The mAb3C9 against O,O-dimethyl OPs (Zhao et al., 2016) and the cyclic eight amino acids random peptide library (Liu et al., 2020) were previously produced in our laboratory. Nitrogen and boron CQDs (NBCQDs) were homemade according to the previous work (Liu et al., 2019) and the typical method was as shown in Supplement materials Method 3.

Electrochemical measurements including Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and EIS were all performed on a model CHI650E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai, China). In this work, the traditional three electrode system was used. The modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE), calomel electrode and platinum electrode were used as the working electrode, reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Screening of mAb3C9 phage-mimotopes by phage display
ce:section.ce:para: The phage display peptide library was performed for three rounds of panning using mAb3C9 immunomagnetic beads (mAb3C9-MB) as target (the schematic of panning process was shown in Fig. S1A, and the preparation method of mAb3C9-MB was detailed in the Supplementary material Method 1). Briefly, for the first round, 10 μL mAb3C9-MB (containing 100 μg of MBs and 4.8 μg of mAb3C9) and 10 μL peptide library (containing 2.0 × 1011 pfu phage particles) were mixed into 1 mL PBS (10 mM). The mixture was incubated at 30 °C with gently shaking for 30 min. Then, the pahge-peptides-mAb3C9-MB complexes were magnetically separated and washed 3 times with PBST (PBS containing 0.1% Tween 20). The phage particles were eluted by 500 μL parathion-methyl solution (100 ng/mL) at 30 °C for 30 min. The eluted phage particles were used for titer determination and amplification for the next round of panning. For the second and third rounds, the input phage particles were 1.0 × 1010 pfu, the washing times were 5 and 7 times, respectively, and the concentration of parathion-methyl solution was 1 ng/mL. After panning, forty individual colonies were randomly picked from the titer plate and rescued by KM13 helper phage. Then, the phage-mimotopes were screened by phage-based magnetic ELISA (P-MELISA) as described in Supplementary materials (Method 2 and Fig. S1B).

The schematic of preparation process of NBCQDs@GO composite was shown in Fig. S2. Firstly, 1 mL ultrapure water containing 1 mg GO was ultrasonicated for 30 min to get GO dispersion liquid. Then, the supernatant of GO dispersion liquid was collected by centrifugation at 9350×g for 10 min. To provide rich carboxyl sites combining with mAb3C9 but avoid affect the electrical conductivity of modified electrode, the concentration of GO (0.0001, 0.001, 0.01, 0.1 and 1.0 mg/mL) was optimized firstly in this study. Then, the NBCQDs@GO was prepared as follows. Different volumes (0.1, 1.0, 10.0, 20.0, 30.0, 40.0 and 50.0 μL) of NBCQDs solution (1 g/L) were added to the optimal GO dispersion liquid, and ultrasonicated for 30 min. The supernatant of NBCQDs@GO dispersion liquid was collected by centrifugation at 9350×g for 10 min. After that, in order to optimize the dosage of NBCQDs, 10 μL NBCQDs@GO (containing different concentration of NBCQDs) was dropped onto the surface of GCE, and the modified GCE was determined by CV, and the optimal concentration of NBCQDs was determined according to the CV curves. The combination of NBCQDs@GO was verified by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), organic element analysis, Raman spectrum and fluorescence scanning.

The phage-mimotope and HRP based electrochemical immunosensor were fabricated according to previous study (Li et al., 2020; Samadi Pakchin et al., 2020) and illustrated in Fig. 1 . Before surface modification, the GCE (d = 3 mm) was polished with alumina slurries (0.3 μm and 0.05 μm, respectively). And then, the excess alumina powder was cleaned by ultrasound in ethanol and ultrapure water, respectively. After the GCE was dried with nitrogen, 10 μL NBCQDs@GO dispersion was drop-casted onto the GCE, then dried at room temperature in air. Then, 10 μL MES buffer (containing 2 mM EDC·HCl and 4 mM sulfo-NHS, pH 6.0) was dropped onto the NBCQDs@GO modified GCE electrode and incubated at room temperature for 2 h to activate the carboxyl group of GO (Miyazaki et al., 2019). After washing with PBS (10 mM) and dring with nitrogen, the activated GCE/NBCQDs@GO electrode was immersed in 100 μL mAb3C9 solution and incubated at 4 °C for overnight. The GCE/NBCQDs@GO/mAb3C9 electrode was washed with 10 mM PBS to remove the unbound mAb3C9. After dried with nitrogen, the GCE/NBCQDs@GO/mAb3C9 electrode was immersed in 200 μL 2% BSA solution to block unbound carboxyl sites incubated at 37 °C for 1 h. The prepared GCE/NBCQDs@GO/mAb3C9/BSA electrode was immersed in a mixture of phage-mimotope (50 μL) and OP (50 μL) and incubated at 37 °C for 1 h. The GCE/NBCQDs@GO/mAb3C9/BSA/phage-mimotope electrode was washed with PBS and dried by nitrogen, followed by immersing in 100 μL anti M13-HRP solution (1/5000, v/v) and incubated at 37 °C for 1 h. The fabrication steps were monitored by CV and EIS.

To improve the performance of the constructed electrochemical immunosensor, the effect of the concentration of 4-CN and H2O2 in 10 mM PBS and the reaction time of substrate on the maximum impedance value (Z′) determined by EIS were optimized in this study. Then, the concentration of mAb3C9 and phage-mimotope, the key influencing factors of the immunosensor, were explored. Based on the ratio of Z (at 100 ng/mL of parathion-methyl) and Z′ (at 0 ng/mL of parathion-methyl), the condition with the smallest Z/Z′ value was selected for further study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and stability of the immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the optimal conditions, the sensitivity of phage-mimotope and HRP based electrochemical immunosensor were evaluated. Calibration curves of 9 O,O-dimethyl OPs were determined and fitted with a four-parameter logistic equation by Origin 8.5. The IC50 and LOD values of each OP were calculated according to the calibration curves. And cross-reactivity (CR) rates were estimated using the following equation:
CR (%) = (IC50 of parathion-methyl/IC50 of other OPs) × 100%

The prepared GCE/NBCQDs@GO/mAb3C9/BSA/phage-mimotope/anti M13-HRP electrodes, used 4 ng/mL parathion-methyl to compete with phage-mimotope M31, were stored in 10 μL of PBS (10 mM) at 4 °C for 0, 1, 3, 7 and 14 d to evaluate the stability of the electrochemical immunosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of spiked vegetable samples by the electrochemical immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: For the recovery studies, three O,O-dimethyl OPs (parathion-methyl, chlorpyrifos-methyl and fenitrothion) were spiked to chopped OPs-free cucumber, cabbage and lettuce samples with levels of 10 and 50 μg/kg. The preparation steps of sample were referred to previous study (Shi et al., 2022). The resuspended solution was diluted with an equal volume of water before analyzing by the developed electrochemical immunosensor. To detect the multi-residues of OPs in vegetables, two or three kinds of OPs were spiked in cucumber, cabbage and lettuce samples, and the level of each OP was 10 μg/kg. The samples were prepared as above.

The phage recoveries of the three rounds of panning were 6.9 × 10−6, 9.4 × 10−5 and 2.1 × 10−3, respectively. The recovery rate of the third round was about 300 times higher than that of the first round, demonstrating effective enrichment of positive phage particles. As shown in Fig. 2 A, with the increase of rounds, the intensity of reaction between phage peptide library and mAb3C9-MB increased, and the reaction was inhibited by 10 ng/mL parathion-methyl. Forty phage particles from the third round were screened by the P-MELISA. As shown in Fig. 2B, the reaction intensity of 10 positive phage-mimotopes showed significant difference at 0 and 10 ng/mL parathion-methyl. The Amax/IC50 values of 10 positive phage-mimotopes were calculated, as shown in Fig. 2C, the Amax/IC50 value of M31 was the highest, so it was selected for further study. In addition, DNA sequences of these mimotopes were determined, and their corresponding amino acid sequences were shown in Table 1 . The amino acid sequences of the 10 mimotopes were different from each other, which indicates that the phage display peptide library used in this study had a good diversity.

GO showed excellent performance for the biosensor due to its abundant carboxyl groups for biomolecules binding and large surface area, so the concentration of GO is an important factor affecting the performance of electrochemical immunosensor. The concentration of GO on GCE has been optimized by comparing the CV of GO modified GCE with different concentrations. With the decrease of GO concentration, the potential difference between redox peaks became smaller (Fig. S3A), and anode peak current (Ipa) increased first and then tend to be stable and the current values without significantly change from 0.00001 to 0.01 mg/mL (Fig. S3B). Therefore, in order to keep a good conductivity of the electrochemical immunosensor and provide sufficient site-binding antibodies, the concentration of GO that was selected for further study was 0.01 mg/mL. To further ensure the conductivity of the modified GCE, the concentration of NBCQDs was optimized, as shown in Fig. S3C, the peak current of the GCE/NBCQDs@GO could be significantly enhanced by the NBCQDs. In particular, the peak current increased significantly at 20 μg/mL of NBCQDs and reached the maximum value at 30 μg/mL. Therefore, the concentration of NBCQDs that was selected to combine with GO was 30 μg/mL. As shown in Fig. S3D, the impedance value of GCE/NBCQDs@GO was greatly reduced compared with that of GCE/GO electrode, which further proved that NBCQDs can improve the conductivity of electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of NBCQDs@GO composite and GCE/NBCQDs@GO
ce:section.ce:para: The GO was noncovalently modified with NBCQDs, confirmed by SEM, organic element analysis, Raman spectroscopy and fluorescence scan. Firstly, the morphology of GO and NBCQDs@GO was characterized by SEM. Fig. S4A and Fig. S4B showed that NBCQDs@GO demonstrated the wrinkled sheet-like structure similar with the typically morphological character of graphene-based materials, proving that NBCQDs@GO maintained the intrinsic properties of GO. And the dispersed liquid of NBCQDs@GO composite material dropped on the GCE electrode presented a complete film layer on the electrode surface that can increase the specific surface area of GO and did not affect the electrode conductivity (Zhou et al., 2018). Furthermore, organic element analysis showed that the N/C percentage of the NBCQDs@GO significantly higher than GO, indicating the successful modification of NBCQDs@GO on the GO surface. The Raman spectra of GO and NBCQDs@GO both have peaks in D-band (1350 cm−1) and G-band (1590 cm−1) positions (Fig. S4C). Besides, the G/D band of NBCQDs@GO became larger than that of GO, revealing the existence of NBCQDs on GO surface, which lead to the increase of defects on GO surface. As shown in Fig. S4D, the fluorescence spectrum of NBCQDs showed an emission peak around 380 nm. However, the emission spectrum of NBCQDs@GO showed significant quenching of the intensity, due to that GO can be used as an effective fluorescence quenching agent (Singh et al., 2018). This phenomenon indicated the successful assembling of NBCQDs on GO.

In order to further compare the effective electron transfer ability in NBCQDs@GO composites, the CV experiments were performed at the NBCQDs@GO modified GCE (GCE/NBCQDs@GO). It can be seen from Fig. 3 A that a pair of obvious redox peaks appeared in the CV curves which ascribed to the redox of [Fe (CN)6 3−], and the potential difference between redox peaks was less than 100 mV at 50 mV s−1, indicating that this was a reversible electrochemical process. In addition, the peak currents of redox peak increased with the increase of scanning speed. To evaluate the kinetics of the electron transfer on GCE/NBCQDs@GO，the redox peak currents with different scanning rate on the voltametric responses have been studied. As shown in Fig. 3B, the redox peak increased gradually with the increase of the scan rate, which indicated a direct electron transfer on the modified electrode surface. What's more, the Ipa and cathodic peak currents (Ipc) showed a good linear relationship with the square root of scanning rate, the linear regression equations are as follows: Ipa (μA) = 4.4132 ν1/2 + 19.675 and Ipc (μA) = - 4.3129 ν1/2 - 16.812, with the correlation coefficients of 0.9808 and 0.9871, respectively, revealed that the electron transfer on GCE/NBCQDs@GO was reversible electrochemical reaction and controlled by diffusion from the electrolyte to the GCE/NBCQDs@GO electrode surface (Bin et al., 2022; Kalyani et al., 2021). In addition, the redox peak currents of GCE/GO electrodes with different scanning rate on the voltametric responses have been studied. As shown in Fig. S5A, the redox peak also increased with the increase of the scan rate. It can be seen from Fig. S5B, the slope value between the square root of peak current and scanning rate measured by GCE/GO electrode was apparently lower than that measured by GCE/NBCQDs@GO electrode, indicating that the electrode surface modified by NBCQDs@GO had faster electron transfer rate and better electrode performance (Lai et al., 2022). Based on all results, the GCE/NBCQDs@GO performed such fast and steady electron communication kinetics which could be used to enhance the sensitivity of electrochemical immunosensor.

The fabrication process of the electrochemical immunosensor was characterized by CV and EIS measurements. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the reduction and oxidation waves of [Fe (CN)6 3−] were exhibited at the naked GCE and the anodic and cathodic peak potential difference was 89 mV, which indicated the better reversible electrochemical reactions at the naked GCE. Moreover, the redox peak current of GCE/NBCQDs@GO electrode increased significantly, and the anodic and cathodic peak potential difference decreased obviously compared with naked GCE. These confirmed that the electron transfer of [Fe (CN)6 3−/4-] enhanced due to the excellent conductivity of NBCQDs@GO. With the fabrication of mAb3C9, BSA, phage-mimotope M31 and anti M13-HRP step-by-step, the redox peak currents gradually decreased and the difference of potentials increased, owing to these immune recognition elements impeded the electron transfer between the [Fe (CN)6 3−/4-] and the electrode. Each immobilization step on GCE electrode was also tested by EIS. As shown in Fig. 4B, the charge transfer resistance increased with the load of mAb3C9, BSA, phage-mimotope M31 and anti M13-HRP gradually. It indicated that these elements were immobilized successfully, and the results of EIS were consistent with CVs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of detection conditions for the electrochemical immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To optimize the performance of the electrochemical immunosensor, the concentration of 4-CN and H2O2, and the reaction time of substrate were optimized firstly. The Z′ value increased firstly and then decreased along with the increase of 4-CN concentration, and the Z′ value was the largest at 1.0 mM of 4-CN (Fig. S6A). The reaction process and electrode surface changes of 4-CD generated from 4-CN catalyzed by HRP and H2O2 were shown in Fig. S6B. H2O2 is an important oxidant in enzymatic reaction, so its concentration plays an important role for the electrochemical immunosensor. The concentration of H2O2 increased from 0.396 to 19.8 mM, and Z′ values also increased all the time, but the value of Z′ remained stable from 19.8 to 495 mM (Fig. S6C). Therefore, 19.8 mM H2O2 was selected for further study. The optimized results of reaction time between HRP and 4-CN substrate were shown in Fig. S6D. The Z′ values increased greatly while the reaction time from 5 to 25 min and the Z′ values tended to be stable from 25 to 40 min, so 30 min was selected as the optimal reaction time of the substrate to keep the stability and accuracy of the measurement.

To obtain the maximum sensitivity of the proposed electrochemical immunosensor, the concentration of the anti-OPs mAb3C9 and phage-mimotope M31 were optimized according to the ratio of Z/Z′ and the conditions with the lowest Z/Z′ value were selected. The Z′ was increased and the value of Z/Z′ values decreased first and then increased with the increase of the concentration of mAb3C9 (Fig. S7A). At 0.625 μg/mL of mAb3C9, 100 ng/mL parathion-methyl showed the highest inhibitory effect to phage-mimotope M31, so this concentration was selected for next study. As shown in Fig. S7B, the Z′ values showed an upward trend and the Z/Z′ values decreased first and then increased with the increase of the concentration of phage-mimotope M31. At 6.25 × 109 pfu/mL, the Z/Z′ value was the lowest, so its concentration was selected for further study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and stability of the electrochemical immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal detection conditions, the sensitivity and cross-reactivity of phage-mimotope and HRP based electrochemical immunosensor and phage-mimotope based P-MELISA for 9 O,O-dimethyl OPs were evaluated. As shown in Table 2 , the IC50 and LOD values of 9 O,O-dimethyl OPs, determined by immunosensor, were 0.989–4.017 ng/mL and 0.003–0.014 ng/mL, respectively. However, the IC50 and LOD values of 9 O,O-dimethyl OPs, determined by P-MELISA, were 1.420–4.937 ng/mL and 0.106–0.692 ng/mL, respectively. The LODs of 9 OPs determined by the electrochemical immunosensor were 13–85 times lower than those of P-MELISA. In addition, the linear range of the electrochemical immunosensor for each O,O-dimethyl OP was very wide (for example, the linear range of parathion-methyl was 0.005–500 ng/mL, Table S2). The cross-reactivity of these two methods showed similar detection spectra, and the cross-reactivity rates determined by the immunosensor and P-MELISA were 81.653–331.648% and 85.072–295.775%, respectively.

In this study, phage-mimotope M31 and HRP played important roles for the signal amplification of the immunosensor. The phage-mimotope M31 and OPs competitively bound to mAb3C9. The bound phage can combine a large number of anti-M13-HRP because its capsid proteins provide thousands of anti-M13 mAb binding sites. Therefore, a large number of HRP was introduced into the immunosensor by using phage-mimotope. Moreover, in the presence of H2O2, HRP can catalyze the substrate 4-chloro-1-naphthol (4-CN) into another compound benzo-4-chlorhexadione (4-CD), which is a kind of insoluble precipitates. These precipitates were deposited on the surface of the electrode and greatly hinder the transmission rate of electrons on the surface of the GCE, thus increasing the impedance of the electrode surface (as shown in the impedance diagram in Fig. 1, the EIS signal in the presence of 4-CN was significantly higher than that in the absence of 4-CN).

Stability is the basic requirement of the electrochemical immunosensor, thus the prepared GCE/NBCQDs@GO/mAb3C9/BSA/phage-mimotope/anti M13-HRP electrodes were stored in PBS at 4 °C for 0, 1, 3, 7 and 14 d to evaluate their stability. After that, electrodes were added by 10 μL of 4-CN substrate and reacted for 30 min, the impedance values (Z′) of electrodes were measured by EIS. As shown in Fig. 5 , the electrochemical response signal of 1–14 d were 96.41–99.98% of 0 d, suggested that the electrochemical immunosensor with good storage stability (Sun et al., 2022; Xing et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the spiked vegetable samples by the electrochemical immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The accuracy and reliability of phage-mimotope and HRP based electrochemical immunosensor were verified by recovery study. As shown in Table 3 , the recovery rates of three OPs were in range of 88.20–112.50% with coefficient of variation from 2.97 to 15.64%. The recovery of the OPs mixtures from spiked samples were ranged from 75.93 to 112.25% with coefficient of variation 3.40–13.37% (Table S1). These results demonstrated that the electrochemical immunosensor performed a potential for sensitive and accurate analysis of OPs in food.

In this study, phage-mimotopes of OPs were screened from the phage display peptide library and used to develop a high-sensitive electrochemical immunosensor. In the immunosensor, the modified material NBCQDs@GO provided sufficient surface area and enhanced the conductivity of the electrode. The phage-mimotope as mimics of CAgs was not only green, safe, but also can enhance the sensitivity of the electrochemical immunosensor. Phage-mimotope can combine a large number of anti-M13 antibodies, which was beneficial to signal amplification. Moreover, 4-CN as the substrate can be oxidized by H2O2 under the catalytic reactions by HRP to produce white precipitation, which greatly amplified the impedance signal. The LODs of 9 OPs determined by the electrochemical immunosensor were 13–85 times lower than those of P-MELISA. Above all, the strategy of electrochemical immunosensor based on phage-mimotope and HRP provided a new potential way for the detection of OPs and other small molecular contaminants.

Ruirui Shi: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Data curation, performed the experiments, and analyzed the data. Wenting Zou: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Data curation, cormal analysis, and analyzed the data. Zhiling Zhao: performed the experiments. Guanqun Wang: performed the experiments. Min Guo: performed the experiments. Shiyun Ai: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Data curation, and analyzed the data. Qing Zhou: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Data curation, and analyzed the data. Fengchun Zhao: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, and analyzed the data. Zhengyou Yang: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, conceived and designed the research, and wrote the paper.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31772060 and 31901778).
